title: Chapter 11: The New Normal
content: The safehouse had a medical bay.

An actual medical bay, with a examination table, supply cabinets, and a sink with running water. Elena stood in the doorway of the room that hadn't existed yesterday, staring at gauze and antiseptic and suture kits like they were artifacts from a golden age.
"This is impossible," she said for the fourth time.
"The System doesn't do possible," Marcus replied from behind her. "It does *impressive*."
"Three hours ago this wall was the back of your closet."
"Three hours ago I was eating canned stew and trying not to die. Things change fast in the apocalypse."

Elena ran her fingers along the supply cabinet. Her touch was almost reverent—the touch of someone who understood exactly how precious medical supplies were now. She opened a cabinet, inventoried the contents, opened another, inventories that too. When she turned around, her eyes were bright with something Marcus hadn't seen in her before: purpose.

"I can work with this," she said. "I can actually *work* with this. The suture kits are professional grade. The antibiotics—" She pulled out a bottle, squinted at the label. "—broad-spectrum, still sealed. This is better than the clinic at my school."
"Take what you need. Your leg—"
"Can wait until I've organized this room. I work better when I'm useful." A faint smile. "Occupational hazard of being the responsible one."

She turned back to the cabinets, and Marcus understood: this was Elena reclaiming agency. In Derek's shadow, she'd been a resource to be managed. In the safehouse, she was a professional with tools and a purpose. The transformation was visible—shoulders squaring, voice steadying, the constant tension in her jaw loosening by degrees.
Sera found him in the hallway, watching Elena work through the open door.
"The nurse is nesting," Sera observed. "Good. She'll be an asset."
"Is that the CEO talking or the zombie queen?"
"Both. They agree on this." Sera leaned against the wall—Shadow Weave blending into the shadows until only her grey eyes were visible. "Marcus. We need to talk about Derek."
The name dropped like a stone in still water.
"He's still outside," Marcus said. "I can hear him sometimes. Scraping."

"He won't leave. The virus preserves obsessive patterns—it's why some zombies return to their homes, their workplaces. Derek's last human obsession was you. Taking Elena. Winning. He'll circle this building until something stronger pulls him away."
"Like the Alpha?"

"Like the Alpha." Sera's jaw tightened. "Which is why we need to eliminate him before the Alpha's call reaches him. A turned human close to us is a beacon. His scent carries markers that other zombies can trace. The longer he stays, the more likely we attract a horde."

Marcus looked out the reinforced window. Four stories below, in the shadow of the building's entrance, he could see the shape of the former Derek Cole—not moving, just standing, grey face tilted up toward the safehouse like a dog waiting for its owner.
He felt nothing. Not satisfaction, not guilt, not even pity. Derek had made his choices. Marcus had made his. The apocalypse didn't care about fairness; it cared about survival.
"Do it," he said.
Sera moved—and Marcus blinked, and she was gone, a whisper of displaced air the only sign she'd been standing beside him. He ran to the window.
Below, the shadow of Derek turned its head toward a blur of black and grey that descended from the building's fire escape. The blur resolved into Sera, Shadow Weave rippling, grey eyes cold.
Derek-zombie lunged.

Sera sidestepped. Her hand closed around his throat—the same move she'd used on the Howlers, controlled and precise. She lifted him off the ground. His legs kicked uselessly. His jaw worked, teeth snapping at air.

She held him there for three seconds. Looking at what remained of his face—the eyes that had once calculated net worth, the jaw that had issued commands, the mouth that had told Elena she was being dramatic.
"These were the last hands that hurt her," Sera said quietly. Not to Derek—he was beyond hearing. To herself. To Elena, somewhere inside. To the universe, which had allowed this man to exist.
She squeezed.
The sound was brief. Wet. Final.
Derek Cole, who had pushed a man into a zombie horde to save himself, who had rationed food while his partner starved, who had raised a crowbar against a woman who trusted him, ceased to exist.
Sera released the body. It crumpled to the pavement like a puppet with severed strings.
She looked up at the window, at Marcus, and her expression was unreadable—not pride, not sorrow, just the flat certainty of someone who'd made a necessary choice.
"Beacon eliminated," she called up.

**[THREAT NEUTRALIZED: Derek Cole (Turned)]**
**[Safehouse perimeter: SECURE]**
**[Zombie-deterrent field active — 50-meter radius]**
**[Affection: Elena Reyes +3 (relief)]**
Marcus leaned his forehead against the cool titanium of the window frame. Behind him, Elena emerged from the medical bay, drawn by the silence.
"Is it done?"
"Yeah." He didn't turn around. "It's done."

Elena came to stand beside him. She looked down at the crumpled shape on the pavement, and for a long moment, her face was a landscape of complicated emotions—grief that wasn't quite grief, relief that wasn't quite relief, closure that wasn't quite closed.
"Thank you," she said finally. "For not making me watch."
"Sera didn't give me much choice. She moved before I could—"

"Thank you for not *wanting* me to watch." Elena's voice was quiet. "Derek would have made me watch. He would have said I needed to see the consequences of my choices. He would have—" She stopped. Breathed. "He's not here anymore. That's what matters."
She walked back to the medical bay. Marcus heard her organizing supplies, the quiet click of cabinet doors, the rustle of packaging. The sounds of someone rebuilding.
That night—Day 11, the eleventh day of the end of the world—the three of them ate dinner together for the first time.

The gourmet meal kit had portions left. Marcus heated them on the safehouse's electric stove—an actual electric stove, powered by a generator that would never run out—and set the table with actual plates, actual forks, actual napkins. The hydroponic garden in the back room was already sprouting—lettuce, herbs, tomatoes growing at impossible speed, the System's thumb on the accelerator of agriculture.

Sera sat at one end of the table, Elena at the other, Marcus in the middle. They ate in near-silence for the first ten minutes, the food too good to interrupt with words. Then Elena set down her fork.
"Can I ask something?"
"Go ahead," Marcus said.
"The System. The quests. The hand-holding for a fortress." She gestured at the titanium walls. "What's the catch?"
"The catch?"
"Nothing this perfect comes free. I've been alive long enough to know that much. You get rewards for romance. For intimacy. For connection. So what's the cost? What does the System *take*?"
Marcus opened his mouth. Closed it.

He'd been so focused on survival—on the next quest, the next reward, the next meal—that he'd never asked the question. The System gave. The System provided. The System made impossible things materialize from nothing.
What did it take?

**[The System takes nothing that is not freely given.]**
**[Every reward is earned through genuine emotional exchange.]**
**[Your effort, your risk, your vulnerability — these are the currency.]**
**[The System does not consume. The System catalyzes.]**
"It says it takes nothing that isn't freely given," Marcus reported.
Elena's eyes narrowed. "And you believe it?"
"I believe I'm alive, fed, and living in a titanium fortress with a zombie queen and a nursing student, so I'm inclined to give it the benefit of the doubt."

Sera, who'd been eating with the precise movements of someone who didn't technically need to but chose to, set down her fork. "I don't trust it either," she said. "But I trust Marcus. And the System's actions so far have been consistent with its stated purpose. That doesn't mean it's honest—it means it's patient."
"Patient for what?"

"I don't know." Sera's grey eyes reflected the overhead light. "But I spent four years as CEO of a company that was constantly trying to be acquired. I learned to recognize when someone was playing a long game. The System is playing a long game. And the best way to understand a long game is to keep playing until you see the shape of it."
The dinner continued. The food was warm. The fortress was secure. And somewhere in the distance, the Alpha's call echoed through the dead city—a sound that was getting closer, one night at a time.
